questions.question: Which macro creates a new Vec with initial values?
questions.explanation: `vec![1, 2, 3]` creates a new Vec with the given values.

questions.question: Vec<T> stores its elements on the heap.
questions.explanation: A Vec allocates its buffer on the heap. The struct itself (pointer, length, capacity) lives on the stack.

questions.question: Add an element to this vector:
questions.explanation: `push` appends an element to the end of the vector.

questions.question: What happens when you access a Vec with an out-of-bounds index using `v[i]`?
questions.explanation: Indexing with `[]` panics on out-of-bounds. Use `.get(i)` for safe access returning `Option<&T>`.

questions.question: Safely access element at index 5, returning None if out of bounds:
questions.code:
let v = vec![1, 2, 3];
let element = v.___(5);
questions.explanation: `.get()` returns `Option<&T>` — `Some(&value)` if present, `None` if out of bounds.

questions.question: You can hold elements of different types directly in a single Vec<T>.
questions.explanation: A Vec holds a single type T. For mixed types, use an enum or trait objects.
